title: Of inquiry requesting the President and directing the Secretary of State to transmit to the House of Representatives any record created on or after January 20, 2025, under the control of the President or the Secretary, respectively, relating to strikes on the Houthis in Yemen and the disclosure of confidential information to a journalist on the Signal application.
plain_summary: This bill asks the President and Secretary of State to give Congress all documents from January 20, 2025 and after about two important topics. The first topic is military strikes against the Houthis, which is a group in Yemen. The second topic is about someone sharing secret information with a journalist named Jeffrey Goldberg through a messaging app called Signal. The House of Representatives wants to see all these documents within fourteen days to understand what happened.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

sections.title: Resolution of Inquiry
sections.text: The House of Representatives asks the President and orders the Secretary of State to send copies of all documents to the House within fourteen days after this resolution passes. These documents must be created on or after January twentieth, two thousand twenty five, and must be controlled by either the President or the Secretary of State. The documents must relate to strikes against the Houthis (a group in Yemen) and the sharing of secret information with a journalist using the Signal messaging app. The House wants all documents, Signal app chats, charts, tables, meeting notes, audio recordings, phone and email records, letters, artificial intelligence conversation records, and any other communications that relate to seven specific topics. First, the complete record of a Signal group chat that included journalist Jeffrey Goldberg and discussed very sensitive and secret plans to attack the Houthis in Yemen, including all messages sent before and after Goldberg joined and left the chat. Second, information about the strikes on the Houthis in Yemen. Third, coordination with partner countries, allies, and other nations about these strikes. Fourth, legal reasons for these strikes. Fifth, any materials created after Goldberg left the group chat and published an article describing how he was included in secret war planning, including any documents about possible consequences for officials who used a commercial app to coordinate war plans, discuss whether to strike the Houthis, and include a journalist in the chat. Sixth, any new executive branch process reforms, safeguards, or protections put in place after senior national security officials invited a journalist to a group chat where highly classified information was discussed, considered, and planned. Seventh, any group chat or transcript that was used to develop war plans or discuss sensitive national security information.
